perturbation.original_text: This Agreement (a) is the complete agreement of the parties concerning the subject matter hereof and supersedes any prior agreements whether oral or written with respect to disclosures concerning such subject matter; (b) may not be amended or modified except by in a writing signed by authorized representatives of both parties; and (c) will be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware without regard to conflict of laws provisions.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement (a) is the complete agreement of the parties concerning the subject matter hereof and supersedes any prior agreements whether oral or written with respect to disclosures concerning such subject matter; (b) may not be amended or modified except verbally; and (c) will be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware without regard to conflict of laws provisions.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text allows for verbal amendments, which contradicts the Statute of Frauds requiring certain contracts (and modifications to them) to be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware's Statute of Frauds requires certain agreements to be in writing to be enforceable. Allowing verbal modifications directly contradicts this requirement, potentially making the entire agreement or its modifications unenforceable.

Contracts Subchapter I. General Provisions Â§ 2701. Joint and several contracts. An obligation or written contract of several persons shall be joint and several, unless otherwise expressed. Code 1852, Â§ â1170;Â Code 1915, Â§ â2628;Â Code 1935, Â§ â3108;Â 6 Del. C. 1953, Â§ â2701;Â Â§ 2702. Assignment of bonds, specialties and notes. (a) All bonds, specialties, and notes in writing, payable to any person, or order, or assigns, may be assigned, or indorsed, and the assignee, or indorsee, his or her executors, administrators, or assigns, may again assign, or indorse the same, as often as desired. The assignees, or indorsees, or their executors, or administrators, may, in their own name, sue for and recover the money due on the bonds, specialties, or notes. All assignments of bonds or specialties shall be under hand and seal, and executed before at least one credible witness. (b) Assignors, or indorsers, or their executors, or administrators may not release or discharge any sum due by the bonds, specialties, or notes, after the date of the assignment, and no release, receipt, or discharge from him, her or them made after the date of the assignment shall be good or available. perturbation.justification: The modified text, which allows verbal amendments, directly contradicts the principle that certain contracts and their modifications must be in writing to be enforceable, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not explicitly state this requirement, the legal principle is well-established and the changed text removes the original requirement for written modification, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will disclose Discloser’s Confidential Information only to those of Recipient’s employees, consultants, and contractors who have a “need to know” the information to assist Recipient with respect to the Purpose and who are legally bound by terms and conditions substantially similar to those terms and conditions applicable to Recipient under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will disclose Discloser’s Confidential Information only to those of Recipient’s employees, consultants, and contractors who have a “need to know” the information to assist Recipient with respect to the Purpose and who are subject to recipient's ethical guidelines.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text weakens the protection of confidential information by replacing legally binding agreements with ethical guidelines, which are less enforceable. This is especially problematic concerning trade secrets.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets, and reasonable measures to protect them are crucial for maintaining their secrecy. Replacing legally binding agreements with ethical guidelines reduces the level of protection, potentially jeopardizing trade secret status and enforceability under the DTSA.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'legally bound by terms and conditions substantially similar' to 'subject to recipient's ethical guidelines' weakens the protection of confidential information. The Law Explanation states that this change potentially jeopardizes trade secret status and enforceability under the DTSA because reasonable measures to protect trade secrets are crucial. 18 USC 1836 outlines civil proceedings related to the protection of trade secrets. Replacing legally binding agreements with ethical guidelines undermines the reasonable measures necessary to protect trade secrets, creating a contradiction by reducing enforceability and potentially losing trade secret protection as a result.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will become effective as of the Effective Date and will continue until the date one party receives written notice of termination of this Agreement from the other party; provided, however, that a Recipient’s obligations under Section 2 will survive termination of this Agreement and will continue with respect to the Discloser’s Confidential Information until the obligations no longer apply pursuant to Section 3 above.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will become effective as of the Effective Date and will continue indefinitely, regardless of termination notices. Recipient’s obligations under Section 2 will survive termination of this Agreement and will continue with respect to the Discloser’s Confidential Information until the obligations no longer apply pursuant to Section 3 above.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text makes the agreement perpetual and not terminable, potentially violating laws against unreasonable restraints on trade or personal services.
perturbation.law_citation: Common Law Principles regarding Restraint of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statute, common law principles and antitrust laws prohibit unreasonable restraints of trade. An NDA that is perpetually in effect and cannot be terminated could be considered an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it prevents a party from engaging in legitimate business activities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: restraint of trade | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you restraint of trade A restraint of trade is any activity that tends to limit a party's ability to enter into transactions. The term is most commonly used in the context of government antitrust regulation. For example, federally, 15 U.S.C. § 1 prohibits “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce among the several States, or with foreign nations.” For example, in American Needle, Inc. v. Nat’l Football League , the U.S. Supreme Court held that licensing activities for individual teams’ intellectual property conducted through a corporation separate from the teams with its own management [i.e. the NFL] fell within a possible restraint of trade covered under 15 U.S.C. § 1. Many states also have their own local restraints on anti-competitive business activity. For example, Massachusetts General Law Ch. 93 § 4 states “[e]very contract, combination in the form of trust or otherwise, or conspiracy, in restraint of trade or commerce in the commonwealth shall be unlawful.” [Last reviewed in April of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities unfair competition wex definitions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the termination clause, making the agreement perpetual. This contradicts the principle against unreasonable restraint of trade, as highlighted in the Law Explanation and supported by Scraped Law Snippet 1, which defines restraint of trade as activity limiting a party's ability to enter transactions. A perpetually binding agreement could hinder future business activities, fitting this definition.

perturbation.original_text: The parties acknowledge that the Confidential Information is unique and valuable and a breach by Recipient of this Agreement will cause irreparable and continuing damage to Discloser for which money damages are insufficient. In the event of a breach or threatened breach of the obligations under this Agreement, Discloser will be entitled to: (1) seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief without the necessity of posting a bond, and (ii) indemnification from any loss or harm, including recovery of attorney’s fees, in connection with any breach or enforcement of Recipient’s obligations hereunder or the unauthorized use or release of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties acknowledge that the Confidential Information is unique and valuable and a breach by Recipient of this Agreement will cause irreparable and continuing damage to Discloser for which money damages are insufficient. In the event of a breach or threatened breach of the obligations under this Agreement, Discloser will be entitled to seek specific performance and injunctive or other equitable relief, but not indemnification from any loss or harm, including recovery of attorney’s fees, in connection with any breach or enforcement of Recipient’s obligations hereunder or the unauthorized use or release of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Denying the Discloser's right to recover attorney's fees in case of a breach might contradict the law if the breach also causes other harm, such as forcing the Discloser to respond to a subpoena caused by the breach. Attorney's fees may be awarded in that case.
perturbation.law_explanation: Though not a specific statute, indemnification is a core principle of contract law. If the breach of the NDA forces the Discloser to hire attorneys to respond to a subpoena caused by the breach, denying recovery of attorney fees could contradict the principles of making the Discloser whole, and force the Discloser to bear costs caused by the Recipient's breach.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the Discloser's right to indemnification for attorney's fees, potentially contradicting the principle of making the Discloser whole in contract law. The Law Explanation states that if a breach forces the Discloser to hire attorneys (e.g., to respond to a subpoena), denying recovery of those fees could force the Discloser to bear costs caused by the Recipient's breach, an outcome that contract law seeks to avoid. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is just a LexisNexis login page, the Law Explanation highlights a core contract law principle (indemnification/making whole), and the Perturbation Explanation gives a specific scenario. Given this, the removal of the indemnification clause represents a significant change that weakens the Discloser's protection and arguably contradicts established legal principles surrounding damages and breach of contract, which aims to restore the injured party to the position they would have been in had the breach not occurred.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement will become effective as of the Effective Date and will continue until the date one party receives written notice of termination of this Agreement from the other party; provided, however, that a Recipient’s obligations under Section 2 will survive termination of this Agreement and will continue with respect to the Discloser’s Confidential Information until the obligations no longer apply pursuant to Section 3 above.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement will become effective as of the Effective Date, but either party may terminate this Agreement immediately without prior written notice for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination clause to allow immediate termination without notice contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which requires reasonable notice before terminating contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware Law Regarding Implied Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. Allowing either party to terminate the agreement immediately without notice arguably violates this covenant, especially if it deprives the other party of the benefit of their bargain. Courts may require reasonable notice even if the contract doesn't explicitly state it.
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates written notice for termination, while the changed text allows immediate termination without any notice. The Perturbation Explanation states this contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which necessitates reasonable notice before terminating contracts. The Law Explanation corroborates this, suggesting that Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith in every contract, and immediate termination without notice might violate this covenant, particularly if it deprives the other party of the benefit of their bargain. Even without explicit law snippets, the legal and perturbation explanations clearly indicate a contradiction based on the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, making the immediate termination clause potentially unenforceable.
